variables.μ: Coefficient of friction between tyre and road (dimensionless)

locked_facts: For banked road: tan(θ) = v²/(rg) at ideal speed
At ideal speed: no friction needed
Below ideal speed: friction acts up the bank
Above ideal speed: friction acts down the bank
Maximum safe speed on banked road with friction: v² = rg(μ+tanθ)/(1-μtanθ)
truth_statements: On a banked road, the component of normal force provides the centripetal force at the ideal speed
At the ideal speed for banking, no frictional force is needed
If the speed is less than ideal, friction acts up the incline to prevent sliding inward
If the speed exceeds the ideal speed, friction acts down the incline
The banking angle depends on the speed of the vehicle and the radius of the curve

aha_moment.what_to_show: Car on banked road at ideal speed. Force diagram shows N decomposed into N·cos(θ) = mg (vertical balance) and N·sin(θ) providing centripetal force (horizontal). No friction arrow present at all.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: N·sin(θ) arrow pointing horizontally toward center labelled 'centripetal force' — and conspicuous ABSENCE of any friction arrow
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students see that banking itself provides the centripetal force via the normal force component — friction is not needed at the ideal speed

simulation_states.label: Setup
simulation_states.description: Car on a flat curved road. Friction provides all centripetal force. Friction arrow shown pointing inward. Speed, radius, and friction coefficient labelled.
simulation_states.emphasis: On a flat road, friction alone must provide centripetal force — this limits maximum safe speed

simulation_states.label: Buildup
simulation_states.description: Road tilts to banking angle θ. Normal force N is no longer vertical — it tilts. N is decomposed into N·cos(θ) (vertical) and N·sin(θ) (horizontal toward center).
simulation_states.emphasis: Banking tilts the normal force so it has a horizontal component — this component points toward the center of the curve

simulation_states.label: Aha
simulation_states.description: At ideal speed v = √(rg·tanθ): N·sin(θ) provides exactly the centripetal force needed. N·cos(θ) = mg. No friction arrow at all. Labels: 'No friction needed!'.
simulation_states.emphasis: At the ideal speed, the normal force component alone provides centripetal force — zero friction required. This is why roads are banked.

simulation_states.label: Explore
simulation_states.description: Interactive: adjust speed and banking angle. Below ideal speed: friction arrow appears pointing UP the bank. Above ideal speed: friction arrow appears pointing DOWN the bank. At ideal speed: friction vanishes.
simulation_states.emphasis: Watch the friction arrow flip direction as you cross the ideal speed. At the ideal speed, it disappears entirely.

common_misconceptions.belief: Banking is only needed when there is no friction — if the road has friction, banking is unnecessary
trigger_phrases: banking sirf friction nahi toh chahiye
if road has friction no need banking
common_misconceptions.correction: NCERT says banking reduces dependence on friction for centripetal force. At the ideal speed, no friction is needed at all. Banking allows safe turning at higher speeds than friction alone could support.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show force components at ideal speed — N·sin(θ) provides centripetal force, no friction arrow present

legend.meaning: Normal force N (perpendicular to banked surface)
Friction (if present — direction depends on speed)

typical_question_types: minimum speed to not slide inward
common_traps: forgetting friction can act up or down the bank
wrong component resolution of normal force
